words.shortDefinition: A lightweight, in-memory representation of the real DOM.
words.longDefinition: A programming concept where an ideal, or 'virtual', representation of a UI is kept in memory and synced with the 'real' DOM by a library such as ReactDOM. This process is called reconciliation.
words.techContext: The foundation of React's performance model, allowing it to batch updates and minimize expensive direct DOM manipulations.
words.exampleSentence: By using a Virtual DOM, the framework calculates the most efficient way to update the UI without re-rendering the entire page.
words.etymology: from 'virtual' (existing in essence but not in physical form) + 'DOM' (Document Object Model).

words.shortDefinition: The algorithm React uses to diff one tree with another to determine which parts need to change.
words.longDefinition: The process through which React updates the DOM. When a component's state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one to calculate the minimum number of changes required to update the real DOM.
words.techContext: Crucial for understanding how React's 'diffing' algorithm works and why things like 'keys' are necessary for performance.
words.exampleSentence: During reconciliation, React identified that only the text content of the list item changed, leaving the rest of the node intact.
words.etymology: from Latin reconciliare ('to bring together again').

words.shortDefinition: A syntax extension for JavaScript that looks like HTML.
words.longDefinition: JavaScript XML. It allows developers to write HTML-like structures directly in JavaScript files. JSX is transpiled into regular JavaScript function calls (like React.createElement) by tools like Babel.
words.techContext: The standard way to define UI components in React, blending markup logic with component behavior.
words.exampleSentence: While JSX is not required for React, it makes the code much more readable by visually representing the UI structure.

words.shortDefinition: Functions that let you 'hook into' React state and lifecycle features from function components.
words.longDefinition: Introduced in React 16.8, Hooks allow developers to use state and other React features without writing a class. Examples include useState for local state and useEffect for side effects.
words.techContext: The modern standard for state management and logic reuse in React, replacing many use cases for HOCs and Render Props.
words.exampleSentence: We refactored our class components to functional components using Hooks to make the logic easier to share across the app.
words.etymology: Metaphorical use of 'hook' to mean intercepting or connecting to internal processes.

words.shortDefinition: The process of attaching event listeners to static HTML returned from the server.
words.longDefinition: In Server-Side Rendering (SSR), the server sends a fully rendered HTML page to the client. Hydration is the process where React runs on the client, matches the HTML with the component tree, and makes the page interactive by attaching event listeners.
words.techContext: Critical for SEO and initial load performance in frameworks like Next.js or Remix.
words.exampleSentence: The page appeared quickly due to SSR, but it wasn't interactive until hydration was complete.
words.etymology: from Greek hydōr ('water') — metaphorically 'watering' dry, static HTML to make it 'live'.

words.shortDefinition: A component that lets you declaratively wait for something to load.
words.longDefinition: A React feature that allows components to 'suspend' rendering while waiting for an asynchronous operation (like data fetching or code splitting) to complete, showing a fallback UI in the meantime.
words.techContext: Used to manage loading states at a high level without manual 'isLoading' flags in every component.
words.exampleSentence: We wrapped the heavy chart component in Suspense to show a loading spinner while the bundle was being fetched.
words.etymology: from Latin suspendere ('to hang up, interrupt').

codeExample.code:
<Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
  <LargeDataGrid />
</Suspense>

words.shortDefinition: A way to pass data through the component tree without prop drilling.
words.longDefinition: Provides a way to share values like themes, user preferences, or authentication status between components without having to explicitly pass a prop through every level of the tree.
words.techContext: Used for global state management when full-blown libraries like Redux might be overkill.
words.exampleSentence: By using the Context API, we managed to provide the current theme to all components regardless of how deep they are in the tree.

words.shortDefinition: A function that takes a component and returns a new component.
words.longDefinition: A pattern for reusing component logic. A Higher-Order Component (HOC) is an advanced technique in React for transforming a component into another with additional props or behavior.
words.techContext: Commonly used for cross-cutting concerns like authentication, logging, or data fetching before Hooks became the standard.
words.exampleSentence: We used an HOC called 'withAuth' to wrap all our protected routes and handle redirect logic in one place.
words.etymology: Modeled after 'higher-order functions' in functional programming.

words.shortDefinition: A technique for sharing code between components using a prop whose value is a function.
words.longDefinition: A component with a render prop takes a function that returns a React element and calls it instead of implementing its own render logic. It allows the component to 'delegate' its rendering to the consumer.
words.techContext: Useful for components that manage state or logic but don't want to dictate the UI structure (e.g., mouse trackers, data fetchers).
words.exampleSentence: The MouseTracker component uses a render prop to let us decide exactly how to display the cursor coordinates.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the pattern: passing a 'render' function as a 'prop'.

codeExample.code:
(

The mouse position is {mouse.x}, {mouse.y}

)} />

words.shortDefinition: A way to group a list of children without adding extra nodes to the DOM.
words.longDefinition: React components must return a single root element. Fragments let you wrap multiple elements together without creating a wrapper <div> or <span> that could break CSS layouts like Flexbox or Grid.
words.techContext: Essential for keeping the DOM tree clean and avoiding 'div soup'.
words.exampleSentence: I used a Fragment to return two table cells from my component, as wrapping them in a div would be invalid HTML inside a row.
words.etymology: from Latin fragmentum ('a piece broken off').

words.shortDefinition: A way to render children into a DOM node that exists outside the hierarchy of the parent component.
words.longDefinition: Portals provide a first-class way to render children into a DOM node that exists outside the DOM hierarchy of the parent component, such as a global modal or a tooltip container.
words.techContext: Critical for UI elements that need to 'break out' of overflow: hidden or z-index constraints of their parents.
words.exampleSentence: We used a Portal to render the modal at the end of the document body so it wouldn't be clipped by the side navigation.

words.shortDefinition: A way to access DOM nodes or React elements created in the render method.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'reference'. Refs provide a way to access the underlying DOM node or a component instance directly. They are often used as an 'escape hatch' from the declarative flow.
words.techContext: Used for managing focus, text selection, triggering animations, or integrating with third-party DOM libraries (like D3).
words.exampleSentence: I created a ref to the input field so I could programmatically focus it when the user clicks the Edit button.

words.shortDefinition: A special string attribute you need to include when creating lists of elements.
words.longDefinition: Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or been removed. They should be given to the elements inside an array to provide a stable identity for the reconciliation algorithm.
words.techContext: Crucial for performance and preventing state bugs when rendering dynamic lists. Using indexes as keys is generally discouraged for dynamic lists.
words.exampleSentence: Don't forget to add a unique key to each list item, otherwise React might incorrectly reuse the state of an old element.
words.etymology: Metaphorical use of 'key' as a unique identifier.

words.shortDefinition: A web standard used to encapsulate CSS and HTML within a web component.
words.longDefinition: Unlike the Virtual DOM (which is a React concept), Shadow DOM is a browser technology that allows a component to have its own 'hidden' DOM tree, which can't be styled by the main document's CSS.
words.techContext: Often confused with Virtual DOM. React doesn't typically use Shadow DOM, though it can work with Web Components that do.
words.exampleSentence: While React uses a Virtual DOM for diffing, Web Components use the Shadow DOM for encapsulation.

words.shortDefinition: Describing WHAT the UI should look like, rather than HOW to change it.
words.longDefinition: In declarative programming, you describe the desired state of the UI, and the framework takes care of the transitions. React updates the DOM to match your component's current state automatically.
words.techContext: The core philosophy of React, contrasting with 'imperative' programming (like jQuery) where you manually tell the browser each step to change the DOM.
words.exampleSentence: React's declarative nature makes our code much easier to debug because we only need to worry about the data, not the DOM state.

words.shortDefinition: The principle that data in an app moves in only one direction.
words.longDefinition: Also known as 'one-way data binding'. In React, data (props) flows down from parent to child. State changes in the child must be communicated back up via callbacks, which then update the state in the parent.
words.techContext: Prevents complex 'spaghetti' state updates where data changes in one part of the app unpredictably affect other parts.
words.exampleSentence: Unidirectional data flow makes it easier to track where data is coming from and which component is responsible for changing it.

words.shortDefinition: Passing data through multiple levels of components that don't need it.
words.longDefinition: A scenario where a piece of state is passed through several layers of components just to reach a deeply nested child that actually needs it, even though the intermediate components have no use for that data.
words.techContext: Often used as a justification for introducing Context API or a state management library like Redux or Zustand.
words.exampleSentence: We were doing way too much prop drilling with the user's profile data, so we moved it into a global Context.
words.etymology: Metaphor for 'drilling' through layers of the component tree.

codeExample.code:
// Prop drilling: passing 'user' through Mid just to get to End
<Root user={user}>
  <Mid user={user}>
    <End user={user} />
  </Mid>
</Root>

words.shortDefinition: React's cross-browser wrapper around the browser's native event.
words.longDefinition: React wraps the browser's native events in a 'SyntheticEvent' instance to ensure that events have the same properties across different browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari). It also implements event pooling for performance.
words.techContext: Important when you need to access 'nativeEvent' or when dealing with event persistence in older versions of React.
words.exampleSentence: Even though I'm in Safari, the 'onChange' event behaves exactly like it does in Chrome thanks to React's synthetic events.

words.shortDefinition: The internal engine of React responsible for scheduling and rendering.
words.longDefinition: A complete rewrite of React's core algorithm (released in v16) designed to enable incremental rendering. It allows React to pause, abort, or reuse work, which is the foundation for Concurrent features.
words.techContext: The 'under the hood' mechanism that allows React to be responsive even during heavy rendering tasks.
words.exampleSentence: React Fiber allows the framework to prioritize user interactions over low-priority background updates.
words.etymology: Metaphorical use of 'fiber' as a basic unit of work (similar to threads but lighter).

words.shortDefinition: An optimization technique that caches the result of an expensive function call.
words.longDefinition: In React, this refers to skipping the re-rendering of a component (via React.memo) or the recalculation of a value (via useMemo) if the inputs (props or dependencies) haven't changed.
words.techContext: Essential for performance tuning in large applications where unnecessary re-renders can cause lag.
words.exampleSentence: We wrapped the data visualization component in React.memo to avoid expensive re-paints when unrelated state changes.

words.shortDefinition: An object that holds information that may change over the lifetime of a component.
words.longDefinition: Internal data managed within a component. Unlike props, which are read-only and passed from above, state is private to the component and can be updated using specific functions (like setState or the setter from useState).
words.techContext: The 'brain' of a component; whenever state changes, React automatically re-renders the component to reflect the new data.
words.exampleSentence: The search bar's state keeps track of the current text the user has typed.

words.shortDefinition: Read-only inputs passed from a parent component to a child component.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'properties'. Props are the mechanism for passing data and event handlers down the component tree. They are immutable from the perspective of the child component.
words.techContext: The primary way components communicate with each other in React's unidirectional data flow.
words.exampleSentence: The ProfileCard component receives the user's name and avatar as props.

words.shortDefinition: The building block of a React application.
words.longDefinition: A self-contained, reusable piece of UI. Components can be either classes or functions. They accept inputs (props) and return React elements describing what should appear on the screen.
words.techContext: The fundamental abstraction in modern web development, allowing complex UIs to be built from simple, testable parts.
words.exampleSentence: Our design system consists of atomic components like Buttons and Inputs that are composed into larger Page layouts.

words.shortDefinition: The series of phases a component goes through from birth to death.
words.longDefinition: The sequence of events that happen from when a component is first rendered (mounting), through updates (updating), to when it is removed from the DOM (unmounting).
words.techContext: In class components, these are explicit methods like componentDidMount. In function components, useEffect is used to handle these phases.
words.exampleSentence: We use the unmounting phase of the lifecycle to clean up active timers and network requests.

words.shortDefinition: A set of features that help React apps stay responsive under heavy load.
words.longDefinition: A rendering mode that allows React to work on multiple versions of the UI at the same time. It can interrupt a long-running render to handle a high-priority event like a user click or typing.
words.techContext: Enables fluid user experiences even when complex computations or data fetching are happening in the background.
words.exampleSentence: With Concurrent Mode enabled, the UI doesn't freeze even when we're rendering a massive list of results.
